title: Toxic foods overview
text: Many foods that are perfectly safe for people are toxic to dogs, and puppies are especially vulnerable because of their small size, so even a small amount can be a big dose. Knowing which foods are dangerous, keeping them out of reach, and making sure everyone in the household (including children and visitors) knows not to share them is an important part of keeping your puppy safe. If you ever suspect your puppy has eaten something toxic, treat it as urgent and call for help right away rather than waiting to see what happens.

title: Chocolate, coffee, and caffeine
text: Chocolate, coffee, and caffeine are toxic because they contain compounds called methylxanthines. These can cause vomiting, diarrhea, restlessness, a racing or abnormal heart rhythm, tremors, seizures, and in severe cases death. The darker and more bitter the chocolate, the more dangerous it is, so baking chocolate and dark chocolate are far worse than milk chocolate by weight. Keep all chocolate, coffee grounds, tea, and caffeine products well away from your puppy.

title: Grapes and raisins
text: Grapes and raisins can cause sudden kidney failure in dogs, and what makes them especially scary is that the toxic dose is unpredictable and can be small. The exact mechanism is still being studied (tartaric acid is a leading suspect), but the danger is well established. There is no known safe amount, so never give grapes, raisins, currants, or foods containing them, and treat any ingestion as a reason to call your vet or a poison hotline immediately.

title: Xylitol
text: Xylitol is one of the most dangerous toxins for dogs and is easy to overlook because it hides in so many products. This sugar substitute appears in sugar-free gum, mints, candy, baked goods, some peanut butters, certain medications, and even some toothpastes. In dogs it can cause a rapid, dangerous drop in blood sugar and, at higher doses, liver failure, sometimes within minutes to hours. Always check labels (it may also be listed as "birch sugar"), and never give your puppy human gum, candy, or any sugar-free product, and double-check peanut butter before using it as a treat.

title: Onions, garlic, chives, and leeks
text: Onions, garlic, chives, and leeks (the allium family) damage a dog's red blood cells and can cause anemia, in any form including raw, cooked, powdered, or dehydrated. Garlic is considered more potent than onion. Because these ingredients are common in seasonings, sauces, baby food, and leftovers, the risk often comes from table scraps and prepared human foods rather than from someone deliberately feeding a clove of garlic. Keep seasoned human food away from your puppy.

title: Nuts, avocado, and fruit pits
text: Several nuts and certain fruits pose problems. Macadamia nuts can cause weakness, vomiting, tremors, and a temporary inability to walk, especially in the hind legs. Other nuts are high in fat and can trigger stomach upset or pancreatitis even when not specifically toxic. Avocado contains a compound (persin) that can upset some animals' stomachs, and the large pit is a choking and obstruction hazard. Fruit pits and apple seeds contain cyanide compounds and should never be eaten, even though the fruit flesh of apples (without seeds) is fine in moderation.

title: Alcohol and raw yeast dough
text: Alcohol and raw yeast dough are both dangerous. Alcohol, including in foods, drinks, and some household products, can cause dangerous drops in blood sugar, blood pressure, and body temperature, along with vomiting, disorientation, and worse. Raw bread dough is doubly hazardous: the warm stomach makes the dough rise and can bloat the stomach painfully, while the fermenting yeast produces alcohol that is then absorbed. Keep rising dough and any alcohol completely out of reach.

title: Fatty, salty, and rich foods; cooked bones
text: Fatty, salty, and rich foods cause more everyday problems than people expect. A sudden large helping of fatty food (drippings, fried foods, fatty meat trimmings) can trigger pancreatitis, a painful and potentially serious inflammation of the pancreas, particularly in small dogs. Very salty foods can cause excessive thirst, urination, and in extreme cases sodium poisoning. Cooked bones are a separate hazard: they can splinter and cause choking, mouth injuries, or dangerous blockages and perforations, so they should never be given.

title: Dairy, corn cobs, and other items
text: Dairy, corn cobs, and a few other items round out the common trouble list. Many dogs are lactose intolerant, so milk, cheese, and ice cream can cause stomach upset and diarrhea even though they aren't strictly toxic. Corn cobs are a notorious cause of intestinal blockage because dogs swallow them whole. Moldy food and compost can contain toxins that cause tremors and seizures, so secure trash and compost bins. When in doubt about any human food, the safe default is simply not to share it.

title: Non-food toxins beyond the kitchen
text: Toxins aren't limited to food. Many houseplants and garden plants (such as sago palm, lilies, and oleander), as well as antifreeze, rodent and slug bait, certain human medications, recreational drugs, and even some sugar-free or nicotine products, are dangerous or deadly to dogs. Cannabis and nicotine in particular can seriously harm a puppy. Puppy-proofing the home and yard to keep these out of reach goes hand in hand with controlling toxic foods.

title: If your puppy eats something toxic
text: If your puppy eats something toxic, act fast and get expert guidance. Contact your veterinarian, the nearest emergency animal hospital, the ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center at 888-426-4435, or the Pet Poison Helpline at 855-764-7661; the poison hotlines are available 24 hours a day, though a consultation fee may apply. Try to have details ready, including what and roughly how much was eaten, when, and your puppy's weight. Do not induce vomiting or give any home remedy unless a veterinary professional specifically tells you to, since for some substances that can make things worse. Quick action saves lives, so when in doubt, call.
